args.code:
import json
import re

# Load all data
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# STEP 1: Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    
    year = None
    for y in year_matches:
        y_int = int(y)
        if 1900 <= y_int <= 2023:
            year = y_int
            break
    
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'title': book['title'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# STEP 2: Process reviews
processed_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            processed_reviews.append({
                'purchase_id': review['purchase_id'],
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'rating': rating
            })
    except:
        continue

# STEP 3: Create mapping and join
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_years:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

# STEP 4: Match reviews to books
matched_data = []
for review in processed_reviews:
    book = book_map.get(review['numeric_id'])
    if book:
        matched_data.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': book['year'],
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': review['rating']
        })

# STEP 5: Calculate decade statistics
from collections import defaultdict
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for item in matched_data:
    decade = item['decade']
    decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# OUTPUT RESULTS
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'processed_reviews': len(processed_reviews),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_data),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_results),
    'top_decades': decade_results[:5]
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load all data
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# STEP 1: Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    
    year = None
    for y in year_matches:
        y_int = int(y)
        if 1900 <= y_int <= 2023:
            year = y_int
            break
    
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'title': book['title'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# STEP 2: Process reviews
processed_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            processed_reviews.append({
                'purchase_id': review['purchase_id'],
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'rating': rating
            })
    except:
        continue

# STEP 3: Create mapping and join
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_years:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

# STEP 4: Match reviews to books
matched_data = []
for review in processed_reviews:
    book = book_map.get(review['numeric_id'])
    if book:
        matched_data.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': book['year'],
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': review['rating']
        })

# STEP 5: Calculate decade statistics
from collections import defaultdict
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for item in matched_data:
    decade = item['decade']
    decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# OUTPUT RESULTS
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'processed_reviews': len(processed_reviews),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_data),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_results),
    'top_decades': decade_results[:5]
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re

# Load books data
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

print('Debugging book year extraction...')
print(f'Total books: {len(books)}')

# Check first few books
success_count = 0
for i, book in enumerate(books[:10]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    print(f"\nBook {i+1}: {book['title']}")
    print(f"  book_id: {book['book_id']}")
    print(f"  details preview: {details[:100]}...")
    
    # Try to find year
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    print(f"  found years: {year_matches}")
    
    if year_matches:
        for y in year_matches:
            y_int = int(y)
            if 1900 <= y_int <= 2023:
                print(f"  SELECTED YEAR: {y_int}")
                success_count += 1
                break

print(f'\nBooks with valid years from first 10: {success_count}')

# Try different regex approach - look for the actual pattern
success_count = 0
for book in books[:20]:
    details = book.get('details', '').lower()
    # Look for "released on" or "published on" followed by date
    match = re.search(r'(released|published).*?\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(2))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            success_count += 1

print(f'Books with valid years from first 20 (pattern match): {success_count}')

__RESULT__:
json.dumps({'debug': 'completed'})

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load books data
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

print('Debugging book year extraction...')
print(f'Total books: {len(books)}')

# Check first few books
success_count = 0
for i, book in enumerate(books[:10]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    print(f"\nBook {i+1}: {book['title']}")
    print(f"  book_id: {book['book_id']}")
    print(f"  details preview: {details[:100]}...")
    
    # Try to find year
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    print(f"  found years: {year_matches}")
    
    if year_matches:
        for y in year_matches:
            y_int = int(y)
            if 1900 <= y_int <= 2023:
                print(f"  SELECTED YEAR: {y_int}")
                success_count += 1
                break

print(f'\nBooks with valid years from first 10: {success_count}')

# Try different regex approach - look for the actual pattern
success_count = 0
for book in books[:20]:
    details = book.get('details', '').lower()
    # Look for "released on" or "published on" followed by date
    match = re.search(r'(released|published).*?\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(2))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            success_count += 1

print(f'Books with valid years from first 20 (pattern match): {success_count}')

__RESULT__:
json.dumps({'debug': 'completed'})

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data file
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Function to extract year from details string
def get_year_from_details(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    
    for year_str in years:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years from all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = get_year_from_details(details)
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print('Books with extractable years:', len(books_with_years))

# Show some examples
if books_with_years:
    print('\nSample books with years:')
    for i, b in enumerate(books_with_years[:5]):
        print(f"  {b['book_id']}: {b['year']} (decade: {b['decade']}s)")

# Now load reviews
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('\nTotal reviews:', len(reviews))
print('Sample review:', reviews[0])

# Match reviews to books
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_years:
    # Extract numeric ID
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

matched = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_map:
                book = book_map[numeric_id]
                matched.append({
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except:
        pass

print('\nMatched reviews:', len(matched))

# Calculate decade statistics
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for item in matched:
    decade = item['decade']
    # Need to track unique books - but we don't have book_id in matched
    # Let's just use decade as key for now
    decades[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])

# Filter and sort
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decades.items():
    if len(stats['ratings']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'count': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade results (top 10):')
for r in decade_results[:10]:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}s: avg={r['avg_rating']:.2f}, ratings={r['count']}")

result = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

    # Extract numeric ID
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

matched = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_map:
                book = book_map[numeric_id]
                matched.append({
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except:
        pass

print('\nMatched reviews:', len(matched))

# Calculate decade statistics
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for item in matched:
    decade = item['decade']
    # Need to track unique books - but we don't have book_id in matched
    # Let's just use decade as key for now
    decades[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])

# Filter and sort
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decades.items():
    if len(stats['ratings']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'count': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade results (top 10):')
for r in decade_results[:10]:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}s: avg={r['avg_rating']:.2f}, ratings={r['count']}")

result = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: {"books_with_years": 0, "matched_reviews": 0, "decades_analyzed": 0, "results": []}

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the full datasets
reviews_data = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']))
books_data = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']))

# Function to extract year from details text
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    # Find all 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for year_str in matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Process books: extract year and create mapping
books_with_years = []
book_id_map = {}
for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        # Extract numeric ID
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            book_info = {
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            }
            books_with_years.append(book_info)
            book_id_map[numeric_id] = book_info

# Process reviews and match to books
matched_reviews = []
for review in reviews_data:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_id_map:
                book = book_id_map[numeric_id]
                matched_reviews.append({
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        continue

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
from collections import defaultdict

decade_info = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in matched_reviews:
    decade_info[review['decade']]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_info[review['decade']]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
decade_averages = []
for decade, data in decade_info.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_averages.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_averages.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Prepare output
output = {
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_reviews),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_averages),
    'results': decade_averages[:10]  # Top 10
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the full datasets
reviews_data = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']))
books_data = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']))

# Function to extract year from details text
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    # Find all 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for year_str in matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Process books: extract year and create mapping
books_with_years = []
book_id_map = {}
for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        # Extract numeric ID
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            book_info = {
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            }
            books_with_years.append(book_info)
            book_id_map[numeric_id] = book_info

# Process reviews and match to books
matched_reviews = []
for review in reviews_data:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_id_map:
                book = book_id_map[numeric_id]
                matched_reviews.append({
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        continue

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
from collections import defaultdict

decade_info = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in matched_reviews:
    decade_info[review['decade']]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_info[review['decade']]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
decade_averages = []
for decade, data in decade_info.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_averages.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_averages.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Prepare output
output = {
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_reviews),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_averages),
    'results': decade_averages[:10]  # Top 10
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Check what we're getting from the books data file
books_data = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']))

# Check a few books and their details
print('Checking first 3 books details...')
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:3]):
    print(f'\nBook {i+1}:')
    print(f"  Title: {book['title']}")
    print(f"  Details: {book.get('details', 'NO DETAILS')[:200]}")
    
    # Try various pattern to find years
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Method 1: Simple year search
    simple_years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    print(f"  Years found (simple): {simple_years}")
    
    # Method 2: Look for published/released
    pub_matches = re.findall(r'(?i)(?:published|released).*?(\d{4})', details)
    print(f"  Years found (pub/release): {pub_matches}")
    
    # Method 3: Look for month + year
    month_matches = re.findall(r'(?i)(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?(\d{4})', details)
    print(f"  Years found (month): {month_matches}")

# Let's try to manually extract years from all books
valid_years = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Try the simple approach again
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_years.append(year)
            break

print(f'\nTotal books with valid years extracted: {len(valid_years)}')
if valid_years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(valid_years)} to {max(valid_years)}')
    print(f'Sample years: {valid_years[:10]}')

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code: import json; import re; books = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24'])); years = []; for book in books: d = book.get('details',''); matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', d); yrs = [int(y) for y in matches if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023]; years.extend(yrs); print('__RESULT__:'); print(json.dumps({'books': len(books), 'years_found': len(years), 'sample_years': years[:10]}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: {"books_with_years": 0, "sample": []}

args.code:
import json
import re

books = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']))

# Extract valid years from book details
valid_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_years.append({'id': book['book_id'], 'year': year})
            break

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_with_years': len(valid_years), 'sample': valid_years[:3]}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

val_args.code:
import json
import re

books = json.load(open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']))

# Extract valid years from book details
valid_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_years.append({'id': book['book_id'], 'year': year})
            break

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_with_years': len(valid_years), 'sample': valid_years[:3]}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

# Test regex directly on this text
text = data[0]['details']
years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', text)
print('\nYears found:', years)

# Check if years are actually found
for y in years:
    print(f'Year string: "{y}", int value: {int(y)}')

    'text_sample': text[:100],
    'years_found': years
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
