print('\nYears found:', years)

# Check if years are actually found
for y in years:
    print(f'Year string: "{y}", int value: {int(y)}')

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps({
    'text_sample': text[:100],
    'years_found': years
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data first
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print('Loaded books data:', len(books_data))
print('Sample book:', books_data[0]['title'])
print('Sample details:', books_data[0]['details'][:100])

# Function to extract year from details string
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    for year_str in year_matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Test on first book
first_year = extract_year(books_data[0]['details'])
print('First book year:', first_year)

# Process all books
books_list = []
for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        # Extract numeric ID
        id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
        if id_match:
            books_list.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'numeric_id': id_match.group(1),
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            })

print('Books with valid years:', len(books_list))
if books_list:
    print('Sample:', books_list[0])

print(json.dumps({

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data first
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print('Loaded books data:', len(books_data))
print('Sample book:', books_data[0]['title'])
print('Sample details:', books_data[0]['details'][:100])

# Function to extract year from details string
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    for year_str in year_matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Test on first book
first_year = extract_year(books_data[0]['details'])
print('First book year:', first_year)

# Process all books
books_list = []
for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        # Extract numeric ID
        id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
        if id_match:
            books_list.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'numeric_id': id_match.group(1),
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            })

print('Books with valid years:', len(books_list))
if books_list:
    print('Sample:', books_list[0])

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'books_with_years': len(books_list),
    'first_book_year': first_year
}))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            books_with_years.append({
                'id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': (year // 10) * 10
            })
            break

print('Books with years extracted:', len(books_with_years))

# Extract numeric IDs to create mapping
book_map = {}
for b in books_with_years:
    m = re.search(r'(\d+)$', b['id'])
    if m:
        book_map[m.group(1)] = b

# Process reviews and match
matched = []
for r in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(r['rating'])
        m = re.search(r'(\d+)$', r['purchase_id'])
        if m:
            num_id = m.group(1)
            if num_id in book_map:
                matched.append({
                    'decade': book_map[num_id]['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except:
        pass

print('Matched reviews:', len(matched))

# Calculate decade stats
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in matched:
    dec = item['decade']
    dec = str(dec) + 's'

__RESULT__:
json.dumps({'books': len(books_with_years), 'reviews': len(matched)})

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the data from files
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for year_str in year_matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            books_with_years.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            })
            break

# Create mapping from numeric ID to book info
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_years:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

# Process reviews and match to books
matched_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_map:
                book = book_map[numeric_id]
                matched_reviews.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        continue

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in matched_reviews:
    decade = review['decade']
    decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Get the best decade
if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
else:
    best_decade = None

# Prepare output
output = {
    'best_decade': best_decade,
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_reviews),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_results),
    'top_results': decade_results[:5]
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the data from files
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    for year_str in year_matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            books_with_years.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade
            })
            break

# Create mapping from numeric ID to book info
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_years:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        book_map[numeric_id] = book

# Process reviews and match to books
matched_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_map:
                book = book_map[numeric_id]
                matched_reviews.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        continue

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in matched_reviews:
    decade = review['decade']
    decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Get the best decade
if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
else:
    best_decade = None

# Prepare output
output = {
    'best_decade': best_decade,
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_reviews),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_results),
    'top_results': decade_results[:5]
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
print('Loading books from:', books_path)

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

print('Total books loaded:', len(books))
print('\nFirst 5 books details:')
for i, book in enumerate(books[:5]):
    print(f"\n{i+1}. Book: {book['title']}")
    print(f"   ID: {book['book_id']}")
    details = book.get('details', '')
    print(f"   Details: {details[:150]}...")
    
    # Try to find years manually
    all_numbers = re.findall(r'\d{4}', details)
    print(f"   All 4-digit numbers: {all_numbers}")
    
    # Check which ones are valid years
    valid_years = []
    for num in all_numbers:
        year = int(num)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_years.append(year)
    print(f"   Valid years: {valid_years}")

# Now let's extract all valid years
print('\n\nExtracting years from ALL books...')
all_valid_years = []
books_with_years = []

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    valid_year = None
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_year = year
            break
    
    if valid_year:
        all_valid_years.append(valid_year)
        decade = (valid_year // 10) * 10
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': valid_year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f'Books with valid years found: {len(books_with_years)}')
if books_with_years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(all_valid_years)} to {max(all_valid_years)}')
    print('Sample:')
    for b in books_with_years[:5]:
        print(f"  {b['book_id']}: {b['year']} ({b['decade']}s)")
else:
    print('NO BOOKS WITH YEARS FOUND - checking why...')
    # Debug: print first book's raw data
    if books:
        raw_details = books[0].get('details', 'NO DETAILS')
        print(f'Raw details field type: {type(raw_details)}')
        print(f'Raw details: {repr(raw_details)}')
        print(f'Repr of first 100 chars: {repr(raw_details[:100])}')

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps({
    'books_analyzed': len(books),
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years)
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the full datasets from the file paths
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24'], 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:22'], 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from books
books_with_years = []
book_id_map = {}

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    
    for year_str in year_matches:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            # Extract numeric part of book_id
            id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
            if id_match:
                numeric_id = id_match.group(1)
                book_info = {
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                    'year': year,
                    'decade': decade
                }
                books_with_years.append(book_info)
                book_id_map[numeric_id] = book_info
            break

# Process reviews and match to books
matched_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        # Extract numeric part of purchase_id
        id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
        if id_match:
            numeric_id = id_match.group(1)
            if numeric_id in book_id_map:
                book = book_id_map[numeric_id]
                matched_reviews.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'decade': book['decade'],
                    'rating': rating
                })
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        continue

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for review in matched_reviews:
    decade = review['decade']
    decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Get the best decade
best_decade = str(decade_results[0]['decade']) + 's' if decade_results else None

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'best_decade': best_decade,
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'matched_reviews': len(matched_reviews),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'decades_analyzed': len(decade_results),
    'top_results': decade_results[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load just a few books to debug
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24'], 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Debug first 3 books in detail
for i, book in enumerate(books[:3]):
    print(f'\n=== BOOK {i+1} ===')
    print(f"Title: {book['title']}")
    print(f"book_id: {book['book_id']}")
    
    details = book.get('details', '')
    print(f"Details (first 200 chars): {details[:200]}")
    
    # Try different regex approaches
    approach1 = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    approach2 = re.findall(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)', details)
    approach3 = re.findall(r'(2004|1996|2012|2013|2014)', details)  # Try specific years we see
    
    print(f"Approach 1 (word boundaries): {approach1}")
    print(f"Approach 2 (no boundaries): {approach2}")
    print(f"Approach 3 (specific years): {approach3}")
    
    # Let's just manually check if we can find '2004' in the string
    if '2004' in details:
        print("CONFIRMED: '2004' appears in details")
        idx = details.find('2004')
        print(f"Position: {idx}")
        # Show context around the year
        start = max(0, idx-20)
        end = min(len(details), idx+30)
        print(f"Context: ...{details[start:end]}...")

# Now let's brute force extract ALL 4-digit combos
print('\n\n=== BRUTE FORCE TEST ===')
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find all 4-digit numbers
    all_4digits = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    
    for num in all_4digits:
        year = int(num)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            books_with_years.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'title': book['title']
            })
            break

print(f'Books with years (brute force): {len(books_with_years)}')
for i, b in enumerate(books_with_years[:10]):
    print(f"  {b['title']}: {b['year']} ({b['decade']}s)")

print('__RESULT__:', json.dumps({
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'first_10': books_with_years[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load just a few books to debug
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:24'], 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Debug first 3 books in detail
for i, book in enumerate(books[:3]):
    print(f'\n=== BOOK {i+1} ===')
    print(f"Title: {book['title']}")
    print(f"book_id: {book['book_id']}")
    
    details = book.get('details', '')
    print(f"Details (first 200 chars): {details[:200]}")
    
    # Try different regex approaches
    approach1 = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    approach2 = re.findall(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)', details)
    approach3 = re.findall(r'(2004|1996|2012|2013|2014)', details)  # Try specific years we see
    
    print(f"Approach 1 (word boundaries): {approach1}")
    print(f"Approach 2 (no boundaries): {approach2}")
    print(f"Approach 3 (specific years): {approach3}")
    
    # Let's just manually check if we can find '2004' in the string
    if '2004' in details:
        print("CONFIRMED: '2004' appears in details")
        idx = details.find('2004')
        print(f"Position: {idx}")
        # Show context around the year
        start = max(0, idx-20)
        end = min(len(details), idx+30)
        print(f"Context: ...{details[start:end]}...")

# Now let's brute force extract ALL 4-digit combos
print('\n\n=== BRUTE FORCE TEST ===')
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find all 4-digit numbers
    all_4digits = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    
    for num in all_4digits:
        year = int(num)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            books_with_years.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'title': book['title']
            })
            break

print(f'Books with years (brute force): {len(books_with_years)}')
for i, b in enumerate(books_with_years[:10]):
    print(f"  {b['title']}: {b['year']} ({b['decade']}s)")

print('__RESULT__:', json.dumps({
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'first_10': books_with_years[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:72']

with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(reviews), 'reviews and', len(books), 'books')

books_with_years = []
book_map = {}

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            numeric_id = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id']).group(1)
            book_info = {'book_id': book['book_id'], 'numeric_id': numeric_id, 'decade': decade}
            books_with_years.append(book_info)
            book_map[numeric_id] = book_info
            break

print('Books with years:', len(books_with_years))

if books_with_years:
    print('Sample:', books_with_years[0])

matched = 0
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)

for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])

var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:72']

with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(reviews), 'reviews and', len(books), 'books')

books_with_years = []
book_map = {}

for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    years = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            numeric_id = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id']).group(1)
            book_info = {'book_id': book['book_id'], 'numeric_id': numeric_id, 'decade': decade}
            books_with_years.append(book_info)
            book_map[numeric_id] = book_info
            break

print('Books with years:', len(books_with_years))

if books_with_years:
    print('Sample:', books_with_years[0])

matched = 0
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)

for review in reviews:
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        numeric_id = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id']).group(1)
        if numeric_id in book_map:
            book = book_map[numeric_id]
            decade_ratings[book['decade']].append(rating)
            matched += 1
    except:
        pass

print('Matched reviews:', matched)

decade_results = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 10:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_results.append({'decade': decade, 'avg': avg, 'count': len(ratings)})

decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg'], reverse=True)

best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

__RESULT__:
print(json.dumps({'best_decade': best_decade, 'decades_found': len(decade_results), 'books': len(books_with_years), 'matches': matched}))
